diagrams.title: Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) - Deadlock State
diagrams.description: A directed graph showing two processes P1 and P2 competing for two resource types R1 and R2, each with single instance, resulting in deadlock
elements: Two process circles: P1 (left), P2 (right) with labels
Two resource rectangles: R1 (top), R2 (bottom) with single instance boxes inside
Assignment edges (solid arrows with arrowheads): P1→R1 (P1 holds R1), P2→R2 (P2 holds R2)
Request edges (dashed/dotted arrows): R2→P1 (P1 requests R2 held by P2), R1→P2 (P2 requests R1 held by P1)
Circular wait path: P1→R1→P2→R2→P1 forming a cycle
Color coding: Processes in red (blocked), Resources in blue, Edges clearly distinguished
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the exact state where deadlock occurs showing circular wait with all four Coffman conditions present. Helps students identify deadlock in resource allocation graphs and understand how request/assignment edges form cycles.

diagrams.title: Process Resource Lifecycle with Prevention Points
diagrams.description: State diagram showing process states (Running, Ready, Blocked) with deadlock prevention strategies applied at each transition point
elements: Three oval states: Running (center), Ready (left), Blocked (right)
Solid arrows for normal transitions: Ready→Running (dispatch), Running→Ready (timeout), Running→Blocked (I/O wait), Blocked→Ready (I/O complete)
Request edge: Running→Blocked when resource unavailable

Dashed boxes at transitions showing prevention methods: At Request - eliminate Hold and Wait, At Release - prevent No Preemption violation, At Acquire - Banker's Algorithm check, Circular wait prevention - resource ordering R1<R2<...
diagrams.purpose: Shows where in the process lifecycle each deadlock prevention/avoidance strategy is applied. Connects theoretical conditions to practical implementation points in the OS.

diagrams.title: Banker's Algorithm Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flowchart showing how Banker's Algorithm determines safe/unsafe states before granting resource requests
elements: Start diamond: New resource request arrives
Decision box: Is Request ≤ Need?
Decision box: Is Request ≤ Available?
Pretend allocation box: Available = Available - Request, Allocation = Allocation + Request, Need = Need - Request
Safety check parallelogram: Initialize Work = Available, Finish[i] = false for all processes
Loop: Find process where Finish[i] = false and Need[i] ≤ Work
Action in loop: Work = Work + Allocation[i], Finish[i] = true, repeat until all Finish true
End diamond: Safe sequence exists? (Yes→Grant request, No→Deny request)
Output: Display safe sequence or unsafe state message
diagrams.purpose: Step-by-step visualization of Banker's Algorithm safety check mechanism. Shows the Work/Finish iteration process and how the algorithm prevents unsafe allocations.

diagrams.title: Deadlock Detection and Recovery Process Flow
diagrams.description: Complete flowchart showing system-level deadlock handling from detection to recovery
elements: Detection trigger rectangle: Periodic checks OR timeout-based checks
Detection algorithms: Wait-for graph (for single instance), Resource Allocation Graph reduction (multiple instances)
Wait-for graph construction: Nodes = processes, Edge = P1→P2 when P1 waits for P2
Detection result diamond: Deadlock detected? (Yes/No)
If Yes - Recovery phase: Process termination box (options: abort all, abort one), Resource preemption box (select victim based on cost), Rollback box (restore to checkpoint)
Recovery decision: After recovery, return to normal operation
If No - Continue normal operation, periodic recheck
diagrams.purpose: Complete workflow from deadlock occurrence through detection to recovery. Emphasizes that detection is always followed by recovery actions in practical systems.

diagrams.title: Coffman Conditions - Venn Diagram
diagrams.description: Four intersecting circles representing each Coffman condition with central overlap showing where deadlock occurs
elements: Circle 1 (top): Mutual Exclusion - label with example: Printer, tape drive
Circle 2 (right): Hold and Wait - label with example: Process holding resource while requesting another
Circle 3 (bottom): No Preemption - label with example: Cannot forcibly take printer from process
Circle 4 (left): Circular Wait - label with example: P1→R1→P2→R2→P1 chain
Central overlapping region (dark): All four conditions present = DEADLOCK
Outer regions: Partial conditions but no deadlock
Visual callout: Breaking ANY ONE condition prevents deadlock
Icons under each circle: Printer icon, holding hands icon, stop sign, circular arrows
diagrams.purpose: Reinforces that deadlock requires all four conditions simultaneously. Visual emphasis that breaking any single condition is sufficient to prevent deadlock - key concept for exam.

charts.title: Deadlock Handling Strategies Comparison
charts.content: Prevention: Eliminates one Coffman condition at system design time - Low runtime overhead, restrictive; Avoidance: Banker's Algorithm maintains safe state during runtime - Moderate overhead, requires prior knowledge; Detection: Regular checking with recovery - Minimal restrictions, recovery overhead high; Strategy selection depends on: System type (batch/interactive), Resource frequency, Process importance, Number of processes
charts.purpose: Compare three main approaches to handle deadlocks with their trade-offs. Helps students choose appropriate strategy based on system requirements.

charts.title: Coffman Conditions with Prevention Methods
charts.content: Mutual Elimination: Use spooling for printers - multiple processes can submit print jobs to spool; Hold and Wait: Request all resources at once OR release before requesting - two approaches; No Preemption: If process holding resource requests another unavailable, release all held resources; Circular Wait: Impose total ordering on all resource types and require processes request resources in increasing order
charts.purpose: Detailed breakdown of each Coffman condition with specific OS-level prevention mechanisms. Essential for exam preparation on deadlock prevention.

charts.title: Deadlock Recovery Decision Tree
charts.content: Deadlock detected → Assess recovery options → Decision criteria: Process priority (lower priority victim), Process execution time (shorter wasted), Resources held (fewer is better), Process type (interactive vs batch), Costo factors → Select victim → Recovery action: Terminate process (abort one/all) OR Preempt resource (rollback + restart) → System continues
charts.purpose: Systematic approach to selecting recovery strategy after deadlock detection. Covers victim selection criteria and recovery actions.

charts.title: Banker's Algorithm Data Structures
charts.content: Available: Vector of m integers showing current available resources of each type; Maximum: n×m matrix showing maximum demand of each process; Allocation: n×m matrix showing currently allocated resources; Need: n×m matrix calculated as Need = Maximum - Allocation; Request: Vector of resource requests from process; Safe Sequence: Order in which processes can complete without deadlock; Algorithm runs safety check before granting any request
charts.purpose: Reference table for Banker's Algorithm data structures. Essential for solving numerical problems in university exams.

branches: Definition: Processes blocked indefinitely, holding resources while waiting for others
System Model: Resources (R1-Rm), Instances, Request-Use-Release cycle
Coffman Conditions (4 necessary conditions): Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
Handling Strategies: Prevention (eliminate one condition), Avoidance (Banker's safe states), Detection (find + recover)
Detection Methods: Wait-for Graph (single instance), RAG Reduction (multiple instances)
Recovery Actions: Process termination (abort one/all), Resource preemption (with rollback), Victim selection criteria
Real-world Examples: Dining Philosophers, Printer/scanner deadlock, Banker's Algorithm in banking systems
mindMaps.purpose: Complete conceptual overview connecting all deadlock-related topics. Useful for exam revision and understanding relationships between conditions, prevention, avoidance, detection, and recovery.

branches: Graph Elements: Processes (circles), Resources (rectangles with instances), Assignment edges (solid), Request edges (dashed)
No Cycle: No deadlock guaranteed; Cycle with single instance per type: Deadlock exists; Cycle with multiple instances: May or may not be deadlock
Wait-for Graph: Derived from RAG by removing resource nodes, keeping only process dependencies
RAG Reduction: Sequentially remove request and assignment edges for processes with all requests satisfiable
Safe State: No cycles in allocation graph; Unsafe State: Cycle exists but not necessarily deadlock yet
mindMaps.purpose: Visual guide for analyzing resource allocation graphs to detect potential or actual deadlocks. Important for university exam problem-solving.
